blueprint_refs: baseline_category_transition_model: "Extract Category from each location token; optionally map to super-category"
policy_form: "Sample a sequence of activity categories using a Markov model (category or super-category)"

evidence.metrics: category_share_mae=0.0010 and transition_divergence=0.0316 are very low while topk_poi_recall is low (0.1277), consistent with matching super-category dynamics but not fine-category/POI identity.

missing-fine-category-generation-model.error_identification: POI recall is poor despite very low category share error and low transition divergence, suggesting the model is getting coarse category flow right but not producing the correct fine categories/POIs.
missing-fine-category-generation-model.root_cause_analysis: The simulator generates only a super-category sequence (sc_seq) and then samples POIs directly within each super-category; it never generates a fine-category sequence nor conditions POI choice on fine category. This collapses many fine categories into the same super-category, making it hard to hit the correct POI tokens even when super-category mix/transition matches.
missing-fine-category-generation-model.correct_approach: Add a fine-category layer: (1) fit fine-category transition model from 2019–2020 (with smoothing via super-category when sparse), (2) during simulate_day sample fine categories then sample POIs within that fine category (not only super-category). Keep super-category multipliers by applying them as priors over fine categories via their mapped super-category.
missing-fine-category-generation-model.key_insight: If the evaluation target is at POI token granularity, generating only higher-level categories will often look good on aggregate mix metrics while failing identity/recall metrics; model the finest target level explicitly.

blueprint_refs: Online OOD context window rule: "For each 2021 target day, construct a 7-day context window using only days strictly earlier than the target day; this window may include earlier 2021 days and may backfill from late 2020; never use target-day or future days."
Fit baseline only on 2019–2020; no online learning from 2021 ground truth during simulation.

evidence.metrics: topk_poi_recall=0.1277 is already low; however this issue is about evaluation validity rather than improving a specific metric.

context-window-includes-2021-ground-trut.error_identification: The simulator uses a 7-day context window, but the implementation appears to draw from the full dataset records without restricting the window to only observable history available at generation time (i.e., it uses ground-truth visits from earlier 2021 days).
context-window-includes-2021-ground-trut.root_cause_analysis: MobilitySimulator.context_features/_context_last_poi pulls visits from dataset.day_records_by_user, which includes ground-truth 2021 sequences. The blueprint allows using past days as “context,” but explicitly as an OOD context construction rule—not as an oracle for true POIs/times. In current code, context features directly inject recent real POIs into candidate sets, effectively using ground-truth history as a strong conditioning signal.
context-window-includes-2021-ground-trut.correct_approach: Separate “available context” from ground truth: during evaluation, context should be built from (a) simulated trajectories of prior days when rolling forward, or (b) an explicitly provided observed prefix stream (if the task provides such observations). If only offline full-day generation is required per day, disable context injection from 2021 ground truth entirely (or restrict backfill to 2020 only). Add a guard: context source must be either 'simulated_history' or '2020_backfill', never '2021_gt'.
context-window-includes-2021-ground-trut.key_insight: Any feature derived from the labeled/evaluation-period ground truth (even if temporally earlier) must be treated as privileged information unless the task explicitly provides it as observed input at inference time.

stop-count-mae-high-from-no-2021-calibra.error_identification: Stop-count error is large (~2 visits/day MAE), indicating daily chain length is not being matched well.
stop-count-mae-high-from-no-2021-calibra.root_cause_analysis: Stop count is sampled by tilting the 2019–2020 per-user PMF to match a scaled mean (theta_stop_count_multiplier). This single scalar mean-tilt may not reproduce 2021’s changed distribution shape (e.g., heavier tails or mass shifts), and the clamp of start_min with `start_min = min(start_min, end_min - (stop_count-1))` plus remaining-budget gap compression can create implicit correlations that distort realized stop counts vs what the PMF intended across users/day-types.
stop-count-mae-high-from-no-2021-calibra.correct_approach: Calibrate a distribution-shape adjustment, not just mean scaling: e.g., add a temperature/dispersion parameter for stop counts (tilt strength) or fit a 2021-validation correction table per daytype (weekday/weekend) at the population level. Also log realized stop_count histogram on V_calib to verify tilt works before time-feasibility adjustments; ensure feasibility logic does not implicitly reduce/increase stops (it currently doesn’t drop stops, but it can force extremely small gaps and end-of-day piling).
stop-count-mae-high-from-no-2021-calibra.key_insight: Matching only the mean of a discrete behavioral distribution is often insufficient under regime shift; add at least one parameter controlling dispersion/tail weight and validate the realized histogram after all feasibility constraints.

poi-recall-low-from-overweighting-generi.error_identification: POI recall@K is low (~0.13), meaning simulated top-K POIs rarely overlap the ground-truth POIs for the same user-day.
poi-recall-low-from-overweighting-generi.root_cause_analysis: POI choice relies on a candidate pool built from (context top, user top, global top, catalog sample) and then mixes preference and distance weights. With calibrated theta_preference_vs_distance_mixture often near 1 (0.944), the model over-exploits high-frequency POIs and repeats (some days show repeated Convenience Store#... or Park#... in samples), which can reduce set overlap when ground truth includes more diverse POIs. Also, lack of explicit “no-repeat” or diminishing returns encourages reselecting the same POI token multiple times within a day, wasting slots that could match additional ground-truth POIs (recall is set-based on simulated prefix).
poi-recall-low-from-overweighting-generi.correct_approach: Add diversity/anti-repeat constraints: (1) maintain a set of already-chosen POIs in the day and penalize/reject repeats beyond a small allowed threshold; (2) introduce a per-day novelty bonus toward POIs not yet visited that day (or a simple self-avoidance factor). Additionally, reduce dominance of global popularity by lowering alpha_global and/or capping ctx_boost so user-specific preferences (2019–2020) are more influential than global hubs.
poi-recall-low-from-overweighting-generi.key_insight: Recall@K for sets is hurt by within-day repetition; enforce diversity or diminishing returns when sampling discrete items for a fixed-length list.

time-of-day-mismatch-from-single-global-.error_identification: Time-of-day distribution divergence is moderate (tod_jsd_avg≈0.056), indicating visit-time placement is not well-matched.
time-of-day-mismatch-from-single-global-.root_cause_analysis: The simulator uses a single global start-time shift parameter for all users/days and then samples gaps from an unconditional gap pool per daytype, with additional remaining-budget compression. This can distort the intra-day temporal structure (e.g., clustering visits late/near end-of-day or compressing gaps) and cannot capture heterogeneous 2021 temporal shifts (weekday/weekend or user-specific changes).
time-of-day-mismatch-from-single-global-.correct_approach: Make time shift conditional (at least by daytype, ideally by user cluster) and fit a gap model conditioned on time-of-day or previous category (as allowed by blueprint). Replace hard budget compression with a forward scheduling model: sample a normalized set of inter-visit proportions (Dirichlet over remaining time) then scale to fit the day, preserving shape while ensuring feasibility.
time-of-day-mismatch-from-single-global-.key_insight: Feasibility clamping after independent gap sampling often reshapes the time distribution; generate time structure in a constraint-aware way rather than fixing it post hoc.

blueprint_refs: radius_of_gyration_error metric requires POI lat/lon; handle missing POIs by excluding those visits

evidence.metrics: trip_distance_wasserstein=0.5255; diagnostics show gt_missing_step_fraction≈0.70 vs sim_missing_step_fraction≈0.62

distance-metric-unstable-due-to-high-coo.error_identification: Trip distance Wasserstein is moderate (0.525) but computed under severe coordinate missingness mismatch between GT and sim, making it noisy and hard to optimize reliably.
distance-metric-unstable-due-to-high-coo.root_cause_analysis: Evaluator mixes paired-step distances when possible, else falls back to marginal steps. With ~70% GT step coordinate missingness and ~62% sim missingness, the set of scored steps differs systematically. This can bias the distance distribution comparison and create unstable gradients for calibration (random search may chase noise).
distance-metric-unstable-due-to-high-coo.correct_approach: Enforce identical missingness policy between GT and sim for this metric: compute distances only on POI tokens that have coordinates in both sequences under an explicit matching rule (e.g., align by step index and require both endpoints in both sequences), and if coverage drops below a threshold, report metric as NaN and exclude from objective for that trial with logged coverage. Alternatively, pre-filter days with sufficient coordinate coverage for distance scoring and calibrate distance only on those.
distance-metric-unstable-due-to-high-coo.key_insight: When a metric depends on optional metadata (coords), calibration becomes unreliable unless you control for missingness symmetry and log coverage/eligibility explicitly.

evidence.metrics: Calibration uses n_iters=15 for 6+ dimensional space; best objective on V_calib=3.054 vs V_test objective=2.935 (no obvious overfit, but likely under-search).

calibration-search-too-small-for-6d-para.error_identification: Overall performance is limited by weak calibration; 15 random trials is unlikely to find good settings across multiple interacting parameters.
calibration-search-too-small-for-6d-para.root_cause_analysis: RandomSearchCalibrator samples a wide hyper-rectangle for 6 core parameters plus multiple super-category multipliers, but only for 15 iterations. With stochasticity from sampling and noisy metrics (especially distance), the best-found point is likely far from optimal.
calibration-search-too-small-for-6d-para.correct_approach: Increase trials substantially (e.g., 200–1000) or switch to a more sample-efficient strategy while staying within current code’s constraints: (1) staged search—first tune stop_count_multiplier and start_time_shift, then distance/mix, then cat multipliers; (2) use low-discrepancy sampling (Halton/Sobol) implemented locally (no new deps) to cover space better than pure RNG.
calibration-search-too-small-for-6d-para.key_insight: High-dimensional calibration with noisy objectives needs either many trials or structured/staged search; small random searches mostly measure noise.
